assertions.description: fetch() remains exposed; the TCP pool experiment is below the Fetch API surface.
XMLHttpRequest remains exposed for legacy clients sharing the same browser socket pools.
WebSocket is exposed; ChromeStatus says normal and WebSocket global socket limits are both studied.
PerformanceResourceTiming is exposed for observing connection timing and reuse signals.
PerformanceResourceTiming exposes connectStart and connectEnd timing fields.
A same-origin fetch produces a resource timing entry with numeric duration and connect fields.
assertions.test: (async()=>{performance.clearResourceTimings();const url='/favicon.ico?tcp-pool-conformance='+Date.now();await fetch(url,{cache:'no-store'});await new Promise(r=>setTimeout(r,80));const entry=performance.getEntriesByName(new URL(url,location.href).href,'resource').at(-1);return !!entry&&typeof entry.duration==='number'&&typeof entry.connectStart==='number'&&typeof entry.connectEnd==='number';})()

assertions.description: performance.getEntriesByType is available for reading resource timing entries.
The browser does not expose a JS-visible setMaxSockets() TCP pool-size setter.
navigator does not expose a connectionPool object for reading or changing the global TCP socket pool.
When Network Information is present, effectiveType remains a connection signal independent of pool sizing.
